paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 14: Snowy (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 2 boy who is in charge of his class hamster Snowy over the September school holidays and accidentally lets her escape, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
For the September holidays my form teacher Mrs Lee picked me to take care of Snowy, our class hamster. I carried the cage home in both hands, sitting carefully on the MRT from Bedok back to my block in Tampines.

Paragraph 2
The first three days went well. I fed her sunflower seeds in the morning and carrot slices at night. I spoke to her sometimes, telling her about my Maths homework. She would sit up on her back legs and look at me as if listening.

Paragraph 3
On Day 4 I was in a hurry. I had promised my friend Daniel I would meet him at the void deck for football at four o'clock. I changed her water bottle, dropped in some seeds, and closed the cage door. I did not check the latch. I grabbed my ball and ran out. Two hours later I came home and walked into the kitchen. The cage door was wide open. Snowy was gone.

Paragraph 4
My heart fell straight down to my shoes. I searched the living room — under the sofa, behind the TV cabinet, inside my mother's slippers. Nothing. I even opened the rice cooker, which was silly, but I was desperate. My mother was at Tekka market. My father was at work.

Paragraph 5
For two hours I crawled on my hands and knees. I called her name in a whisper, because I did not want the neighbours to know. By the time the sky turned orange, I was imagining what I would say to Mrs Lee on Monday. Twenty-eight pairs of eyes would look at me.

Paragraph 6
Then I heard a tiny scratching behind the washing machine. I lay flat and looked. Two black eyes shone back. Snowy. I reached in with a finger, and she walked onto my hand as if nothing had happened. I almost cried with relief. Then I noticed beside her the power cable, with the plastic casing chewed clean through. The wire inside was bare but not damaged. She had stopped just in time.

Paragraph 7
I did not tell my parents that evening. I latched the cage carefully, twice, every time after that. I told my mother only on Sunday night. She sighed and said, 'Next time, you tell me when it happens. Not three days later.' She did not punish me. She did not need to.

Paragraph 8
On Monday I returned Snowy to Mrs Lee's class. The other children crowded around the cage, asking whether she had enjoyed her holiday. 'Yes,' I said. 'She was very good.' Nobody knew. But I knew. From that week on, I checked the latch on every cage, every box, and every door I closed — twice, and then once more for safety.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past exam. MRT: Mass Rapid Transit; void deck: the open ground floor of an HDB public-housing block; Tekka: a market in Little India.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 2, name one food the narrator gave Snowy in the morning. [1]
Q2. What does the word 'desperate' (Paragraph 4) suggest about how the narrator felt as he searched? [1]
questions.answer: he was anxious and willing to try anything / had almost lost hope

questions.stem: Q3. What does the phrase 'in a whisper' (Paragraph 5) suggest about how the narrator was searching? [1]
questions.answer: quietly and secretly, so no one would hear / notice

questions.stem: Q4. Using your own words, explain why the narrator forgot to check the latch on the cage. (Paragraph 3) [2]
questions.answer: ① he was rushing because he had promised to meet Daniel for football; ② in his hurry he closed the door but did not stop to check it was locked

questions.stem: Q5. Using your own words, explain why the narrator did not want the neighbours to know. (Paragraph 5) [2]
questions.answer: ① he felt embarrassed about losing the class hamster; ② he was afraid of being found out and judged, so he searched secretly

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain why it matters that the chewed wire was 'bare but not damaged' (Paragraph 6). [2]
questions.answer: ① Snowy came very close to a real danger / being electrocuted; ② she stopped just in time, so the narrator realises how serious his carelessness could have been

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the phrase 'My heart fell straight down to my shoes' (Paragraph 4)? [2]
questions.answer: it is an exaggeration / image that makes his shock and dismay vivid — it shows how suddenly and deeply his spirits dropped when he saw the empty cage

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the week with Snowy unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: careless / panicked / relieved / wiser / proud / suspicious / annoyed / bored. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Snowy' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the week unfolds — from careless on Day 4 when he is in a hurry for football and does not check the latch, to panicked when he returns to find the cage door wide open and crawls around the flat for two hours, to relieved when he finally hears the scratching behind the washing machine and Snowy walks onto his finger, ending wiser on Monday as he returns her to school and from then on checks every latch twice and then once more for safety. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 as he rushes to feed Snowy, closes the cage door without checking the latch, grabs his ball and runs out to meet Daniel at the void deck? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4 and 5 as he searches the flat on his hands and knees for two hours, even checking inside the rice cooker, and starts to imagine telling the class on Monday? [1]

Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 when he hears the tiny scratching sound behind the washing machine, sees Snowy's black eyes shining back, and she walks onto his finger as if nothing had happened? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 on Monday, after returning Snowy safely to Mrs Lee's class, knowing that he now checks every latch twice and then once more for safety? [1]
